column4: Question
column6: 1 - Lagging
column7: 2 - Developing
column8: 3 - Proficient
column9: 4 - Exemplary

column4: How predictable and consistent is the team's test execution across sprints, from planning through delivery?
column6: Team throughput is unpredictable; test estimates are unreliable, causing frequent scope adjustments and missed sprint commitments.
column7: Attempts to improve velocity and planning but lacks consistency; some sprints meet targets while others miss significantly.
column8: Maintains consistent testing velocity with reliable capacity planning; team completes planned test execution within sprint timeframes.
column9: Drives continuous velocity improvements through predictive planning models; consistently exceeds sprint testing targets with data-driven forecasts.

column4: How effective is the supervisor at ensuring the team surfaces high-impact defects during testing rather than letting them escape to production?
column6: Team findings lack rigor; critical defects regularly escape to production due to weak detection standards and poor test design.
column7: Attempts to improve detection quality but standards are inconsistently applied; some high-severity defects slip through testing.
column8: Ensures team applies consistent quality standards in defect detection; critical issues are reliably identified through well-structured test coverage.
column9: Has instituted rigorous detection processes with sophisticated test design; team proactively surfaces subtle, high-impact issues before they reach customers.

column4: Does the supervisor allocate testers based on their strengths and manage workload so the team stays balanced, productive, and free from burnout?
column6: Talent is misaligned; testers are generalists performing poorly with no clear specialization. Workload is chronically unbalanced, causing burnout or idle time.
column7: Begins developing specialization but assignments lack rationale; workload distribution is uneven with some members carrying disproportionate loads.
column8: Strategically allocates testers based on platform expertise; monitors and balances workload to sustain quality and prevent burnout.
column9: Continuously refines allocation to maximize strengths; proactively manages capacity with pipeline visibility, optimizing workload while protecting well-being.

column4: Does the supervisor foster a culture of continuous improvement in testing tools and processes, pushing the team beyond current practices toward more efficient, innovative ways of working?
column6: Team uses outdated tools and processes; no effort to improve or innovate testing practices. Status quo is accepted even when it limits productivity.
column7: Addresses tooling and process issues reactively; improvements lag behind team needs and emerging testing challenges.
column8: Actively drives improvements to testing tools and workflows; team regularly adopts better practices that raise productivity.
column9: Champions a culture of continuous innovation; team proactively experiments with cutting-edge tools and processes that deliver measurable efficiency gains.

column4: To what extent does the supervisor own the release readiness decision — setting clear criteria and holding the line even under delivery pressure?
column6: Quality gates are weak or ignored; releases proceed without clear criteria, resulting in defect-prone deployments.
column7: Establishes release criteria but inconsistently enforces them; some releases proceed despite unresolved quality risks.
column8: Maintains effective release gates with clear criteria; only releases meeting quality standards proceed to production.
column9: Champions rigorous release gatekeeping with well-defined criteria and unwavering enforcement; ensures only high-quality releases reach customers.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor communicate quality status and testing findings so stakeholders can make confident release decisions?
column6: Stakeholders lack visibility into testing progress, risks, or findings; reports are unclear or missing entirely.
column7: Communicates status irregularly or without sufficient context; stakeholders often misunderstand coverage or risk levels.
column8: Provides clear, regular quality status updates; stakeholders understand testing progress, coverage, and readiness for release.
column9: Produces crystal-clear reports tailored to audience; stakeholders have real-time visibility and full confidence in quality assessments.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor own production incidents end-to-end — from rapid response and investigation support to rigorous root cause analysis that improves testing practices?
column6: Response to production incidents is slow or absent; defects escape without learning. No meaningful RCA or process improvement occurs.
column7: Attempts to respond to incidents and conduct RCA but analysis is surface-level; team identifies symptoms but not underlying process gaps.
column8: Responds promptly to incidents and conducts thorough RCA; identifies gaps and implements preventive measures that improve testing.
column9: Drives comprehensive end-to-end incident ownership; RCA processes uncover systemic issues and permanently improve testing practices.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor protect the team's test readiness — ensuring requirement clarity and timely test case reviews before execution begins?
column6: Requirements are ambiguous; test execution starts without clear, reviewed coverage. Team wastes effort testing incomplete specifications.
column7: Works toward clarity but requirements and reviews are often delayed or incomplete; team must guess at intent or rework coverage.
column8: Ensures requirements are clear and test cases are reviewed on time; team starts each sprint with approved, well-understood coverage.
column9: Establishes requirement review discipline that guarantees clarity; rapid review turnaround keeps the team unblocked and confident.

column4: How proactively does the supervisor clear friction and unblock the team during test execution?
column6: Testing is frequently stalled by unresolved blockers; team operates with persistent friction and no intervention from the supervisor.
column7: Attempts to unblock team but resolution is slow; blockers accumulate and degrade velocity.
column8: Actively identifies and removes blockers; team maintains momentum despite external dependencies.
column9: Proactively prevents blockers through creative problem-solving and rapid resolution; team has minimal friction in execution.

column4: Does the supervisor champion excellence in test design and documentation — producing test cases that are thorough, well-written, and clearly linked to requirements?
column6: No test design standards; test cases lack rigor, clarity, or coverage of critical paths. Documentation and traceability are missing.
column7: Attempts to improve test design but standards are inconsistent; documentation has gaps and some tests lack requirement linkage.
column8: Establishes clear test design and documentation standards; team produces well-structured, comprehensive, traceable test cases.
column9: Champions excellence in test craftsmanship; team produces sophisticated, maintainable test cases with comprehensive documentation throughout the lifecycle.

column4: To what extent does the supervisor turn review and mentorship into a continuous improvement loop — raising both test quality and tester capability?
column6: Does not review team members' work; quality issues go uncorrected and growth opportunities are missed entirely.
column7: Conducts reviews but feedback is infrequent or superficial; team improvement is slow and uneven.
column8: Conducts regular, constructive reviews; team members receive feedback that improves both their testing quality and skills.
column9: Drives a mentorship culture through rigorous reviews; team members grow continuously and maintain high standards through peer learning.

column4: Does the supervisor maintain a disciplined approach to test debt — surfacing risk, reducing existing coverage gaps, and preventing new debt through clear standards?
column6: Test debt is invisible and unmanaged; untested areas accumulate while shortcuts become standard practice.
column7: Sporadically identifies debt but tracking is incomplete; some time is allocated but new shortcuts continue to accumulate.
column8: Maintains visibility of test debt, allocates capacity for reduction, and enforces standards that prevent new debt.
column9: Systematically tracks and communicates debt; strategically eliminates coverage gaps and drives a culture that prevents new debt.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor ensure the team shares expertise internally and continuously evolves its testing skills to match changing technology?
column6: Expertise is siloed; knowledge sharing is absent. Testing approaches remain static despite changing technology.
column7: Encourages sharing but systems are ad-hoc; skill evolution is slow and the team lags in emerging QA practices.
column8: Establishes knowledge sharing practices and guides skill evolution; team learns from each other and keeps pace with technology.
column9: Drives a knowledge-sharing culture with documented practices; team expertise is distributed, continuously evolving, and ahead of industry trends.

column4: Does the supervisor foster a psychologically safe environment where team members speak openly, trust leadership, and stay energized about their work?
column6: Blame culture prevails; team members fear failure or speaking up. Morale is low and trust is absent.
column7: Attempts to build safety but team remains cautious; morale is mixed and trust is inconsistent.
column8: Establishes psychological safety and trust; team speaks openly, takes calculated risks, and maintains positive energy.
column9: Champions deep psychological safety through authentic leadership; team thrives, innovates, and supports each other with consistently high morale.

column4: Does the supervisor's environment produce engaged, loyal team members who stay and perform consistently?
column6: High turnover; team members leave due to lack of development, engagement, or support. Quiet quitting is visible.
column7: Some turnover; team members are moderately engaged but retention could improve and enthusiasm is low.
column8: Team members are engaged and stay; retention is stable with low unexpected departures.
column9: Team is highly engaged and loyal; retention is excellent, morale is consistently high, and the team attracts internal transfers.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor institutionalize QA learnings and broadcast quality insights to other divisions that can act on them?
column6: QA knowledge is lost over time; no documentation or sharing beyond the team. Other divisions miss learning from QA discoveries.
column7: Maintains some documentation but knowledge is scattered; cross-division sharing is minimal and inconsistent.
column8: Maintains organized QA knowledge base and actively shares insights across divisions; other teams benefit from QA learnings.
column9: Builds comprehensive knowledge repository; drives cross-division quality visibility that shapes product decisions across the organization.

column4: To what extent does the supervisor turn the product vision into actionable QA strategy while ensuring the team understands the 'why' behind their testing?
column6: QA is disconnected from product strategy; team sees work as mechanical with no understanding of business impact.
column7: Attempts to align with product vision but understanding is superficial; team engagement with purpose is lukewarm.
column8: Translates product vision into clear QA strategy; team understands why their testing matters and focuses on strategic priorities.
column9: Masterfully embeds product vision into QA strategy; team is laser-focused on customer impact and deeply motivated by their purpose.

column4: Does the supervisor proactively launch quality initiatives and quantify their impact so leadership can see the value QA delivers?
column6: QA remains reactive and tactical; no strategic initiatives are launched. Impact is unmeasured and stakeholders cannot assess QA value.
column7: Launches occasional initiatives but they lack follow-through; impact metrics are incomplete or unclear.
column8: Launches and drives strategic QA initiatives; measures impact with clear metrics that stakeholders understand.
column9: Drives transformative quality initiatives with comprehensive metrics; team's contributions demonstrably drive business outcomes.

column4: Does the supervisor consistently apply risk-based thinking to prioritize testing and triage — ensuring the team spends its time on the most important quality concerns?
column6: No clear prioritization; team spends effort on low-impact defects while critical issues accumulate. Testing is scattered.
column7: Attempts prioritization but criteria are unclear; some high-impact issues are deprioritized or high-risk areas lack focus.
column8: Establishes clear risk-based criteria; team focuses testing and triage on high-impact features and critical defects.
column9: Masters risk-based decision making; team strategically focuses on highest-risk, highest-value areas with rigorous triage.

column4: To what extent does the supervisor make testing trade-offs visible and defensible to stakeholders, especially when coverage must be reduced?
column6: Trade-offs are invisible; stakeholders are unaware of coverage gaps or risks being accepted.
column7: Communicates trade-offs occasionally; stakeholders sometimes don't understand coverage limitations or their implications.
column8: Transparently communicates testing trade-offs; stakeholders understand coverage decisions and associated risks.
column9: Ensures crystal-clear trade-off communication; leadership can make fully informed decisions about quality risks with confidence.

column4: How effectively does the supervisor stay responsive to stakeholders and close the feedback loop so input translates into QA improvements?
column6: Responds slowly to requests; feedback loops are absent. Team's input is ignored and stakeholder improvement suggestions are lost.
column7: Responds within reasonable timeframe but feedback loops are loose; some valuable input is not acted on.
column8: Responds promptly and establishes effective feedback loops; stakeholder and team input drives continuous QA improvement.
column9: Ensures rapid responsiveness; creates virtuous feedback cycles where input is systematically captured and drives strategic improvements.

column4: To what extent does the supervisor grow the team's automation maturity so it delivers measurable productivity and coverage gains over time?
column6: No automation capability; team relies heavily on manual testing with no path toward scalable coverage.
column7: Builds automation foundation but maturity is limited; manual testing still dominates and automation is fragile.
column8: Develops solid automation capability; team balances manual and automated testing effectively with maintainable scripts.
column9: Champions test automation maturity; team has sophisticated, sustainable automation that scales with the product and continuously evolves.

column4: Does the supervisor identify skill gaps in the team and drive targeted development plans that close them?
column6: Skill gaps are unidentified; team struggles with required testing tasks. No development plans exist.
column7: Identifies skill gaps but closure plans are incomplete; team continues to struggle in weak areas.
column8: Identifies skill gaps and develops clear plans to address them; team capability steadily improves.
column9: Systematically closes skill gaps through targeted development; team masters required capabilities and is future-proofed.

column4: Does the supervisor articulate a clear QA vision and lead the team through change smoothly — balancing stability with adaptability?
column6: No clear QA vision; team resists change and direction is reactive. Change is either chaotic or nonexistent.
column7: Attempts to articulate vision but it's vague; change adoption is slow and the team feels unstable during transitions.
column8: Articulates clear QA vision and drives change adoption; team embraces new approaches while maintaining stability.
column9: Commands compelling QA vision; team rapidly adopts changes with minimal disruption and is both reliable and highly adaptive.

column4: Does the supervisor deliberately empower team members by delegating real decisions, trusting their judgment, and giving them space to lead their own work?
column6: Decisions are centralized; team members lack autonomy and wait for direction on everything.
column7: Begins to empower but team members lack confidence; some decisions are pushed down but guidance is insufficient.
column8: Empowers team members to make decisions; team has meaningful autonomy and confidence in their judgment.
column9: Deeply empowers and mentors team members; team thrives with high autonomy, strong guidance, and members grow into leadership.

column3: Culture of Experimentation & Creative Problem Solving
column4: To what extent does the supervisor encourage the team to think beyond standard approaches — creating space for experimentation and rewarding creative problem solving?
column6: Team uses only conventional approaches; experimentation is discouraged and innovation is stifled.
column7: Allows some experimentation but risk-aversion remains; new approaches are not systematically tried or supported.
column8: Fosters experimentation and creative problem solving; team tests new ideas and learns from attempts.
column9: Creates a fearless experimentation culture; team innovates boldly, rewards creative solutions, and treats failed experiments as valuable data.
